Members who % called up an Extra-ordinary

called up by requisitions do not require
an explanatory statement to be annexed
with for the matters regarding which such
meeting should have been called up.

It also provides that EGM when called
up it must be signed by all the members
requesting to conduct a meeting or by one
person who is authorised to sign on
behalf of all other member.

Also, if quorum is not present in the
meeting called up by requisitions shall
stand cancelled.

(i) Here, Board of Directors if co. refuses to convene
EGM on the contention to not give an expl-
natory statement is not valid as per above
mentioned provisions of Sec. 101.

EGM on the ground that requisition have not
been signed by Joint shareholders, is also

And 64 Wds per Section 127 of the Companies Act, 2013, A company declare interim dividend out of the surplus profits of preceeding year and from the profits of the quarter immediately the financial year in which such dividend is declared but before holding an Annual General Meeting.

In the instant case, Sun Dept Ltd. declared Interim Dividend @ 20% in a meeting i.e. on 7th July 2020, which is before its Annual General Meeting.

Thus, Sun Dept Ltd. is well in compliance with due diligence in declaring Interim Dividend.

(ii) As per Sec. 8 of the Companies Act, 2013, companies are formed to promote charitable objects education, sports, literature, science, culture etc. are strictly prohibited to declare dividend. As, such companies intended to apply its profits in promoting its objects.

(iii) If company fails to pay interim dividend, then company shall be punishable with imprisonment upto 2 years, along with a fine of not less than 1000 everyday if the default continues and may go upto ₹ 500,000.

exchange can be done as per Foreign

withdrawal of foreign exchange as per 3 
different Schedules which states:

1. Schedule-I - Schedules-I prescribes 
the details of such transactions which 
are strictly prohibited.

2. Schedule-II - these transactions may 
require prior approval of CG, for draw- 
al of foreign exchange.

3. Schedule-III - these transactions require 
prior approval of Reserve Bank of India 
(RBI) if a person exceeds the limit of 
USD 2,50,000 under LRS i.e. Liberalised 
Remittance Scheme.

In the given case,

1) Mr. Shivesh who requires 50,000 USD 
out of lottery winnings for remittance 
outside India is strictly 'prohibited' 
as the same is mentioned under 
Schedule - I of Current Account 
Transactions' Rules, 2000

financial position.
However, if a company act is prospectus
thus contains any false or misleading
statements and due to such statements
any person suffers losses shall be liable
to pay for the damages claimed by

Ans: 1(c)

Ans 2(A) As per Sec. 68 of the Companies Act, 2013. A company can buy-back its own shares out of:

i) Free Reserves
ii) Securities Premium &
iii) Proceeds of Fresh Issue of Shares.

However, Revaluation Reserve shall not be considered for the purpose of Buy-Back.

Circumstances in which a company is prohibited to Buy-Back its own shares:

1) A company cannot buy-back of same class or kind of equity shares previously issued before Buy-Back.

8. It cannot Buy-Back Shares in a period less than one year.

7. Shares Bought Back shall not be partly paid up.

4. Buyback shall not exceed 10% of paid-up Share Capital and Free Reserve.

5. Infrastructure Development Reserve & Statutory Reserves shall not used for the purpose of Buy Back.

Ans 2(B) As per Section 139 of the companies Act, 2013, An Auditor is to be appointed by Board of Directors in consultation with the shareholders in an annual general meeting. However, as per other relevant provisions of relevant Section of this Act prescribes that remuneration of an Auditor is to be decided by Board of Directors in a general meeting or in such manner as may be prescribed in rules be decided.

As per the facts of the case and the above stated provisions where the H.P Software Private Limited appointed a its statutory auditors but remuneration is not fixed.

By applying above relevant provisions, Board of Directors of H.P Software Private Limited can fix the remuneration or decide the manner as it may be prescribed in general meeting.

Ans (c.c) As per Sec. 15 of the Limited Liability Partnership 
Act, 2008. of an LLP may be if 
The name of LLP in the opinion of Central 
government is not 
i) undesirable, 
ii) too nearly resembles, or 
iii) is similar with the other company or other 
or with the Trademark of the person register 
ed under Trademark Act, 1999. 
Further 
As per Sec. 17 of the Limited Liability 
Partnership Act, 2008. If the name of a 
company or LLP is 
i) too nearly resembles or 
ii) similar with other company or LLP or 
with a Registered Trademark under Trade- 
mark Act 1999) 
then on an application made by the 
company whose name is similar or by 
the Registered Trademark's owner. C.A. ROC 
may order to a change its name within 
a period of 3 months of such application. 

In the instant case, where M/s Laxdhman 
Steels requested the ROC that the name 
of LLP is too nearly resembles with 
name of LLP i.e. M/s Laxdhman Steel 
LLP. and requested him to issue such order 
against LLP to change its name. 
M/s Laxdhman Steels LLP is not 
liable to change its name because 
as aforesaid provision states that

name can be changed only if it is identical
with any other LLP or company or trade
mark, but not with the "Partnership
firm."

Ans 3.A Section 83 of the Companies Act, 2013 empowers
the registrar to suo-moto record
the satisfaction of charge against any such
property or undertaking if the charge
holder and the company upon whose
assets charge was created fails to file
the satisfaction of charge.
Registrar do register the charge only
if it gets any intimation from the charge
registrant, that such property or under-
taking is free from charge (either partly
or fully) and or the title deeds are free
from charge or dues have been settled.
In such a case, Registrar may
issue a Show-cause Notice to to the
the company within a period of 14 days
asking why the charge is not to be
recorded by it.
However, if the request is made by
other person(s) to satisfy the charge and
such request bears sign of the charge
holder or the company, then Registrar
does not issue such notice.
He himself records the satisfaction of
charge, issues a memorandum of
satisfaction
Also inform both the parties

and issues ‘CH-5’

Mr. Ranjit can file ‘CHG-4’ to Registrar
for Registrar applying above provision
‘Suo Moto’, register the charge is
satisfied.

Ans 3(B) As per the relevant provisions of the
Companies Act, 2013, if a company holds
shares or securities in other company
either partly paid-up or fully paid-up,
in its holding co. or its subsidiary or
an associate or subsidiary of such
holding co.

Such companies are treated as
Related Parties as per the definition
of ‘Related Party’.

In the given case, ABC Private Ltd has
two wholly owned Subsidiary companies.
Thus, referring above provision to Private
Limited & Private Ltd. will be treated
as ‘Related Parties of ABC Private Limited.

in India whether ‘main server’
is installed in India or not.

Ans 5. B.

As per Foreign Exchange Management Act 1999, the drawal of foreign exchange can be done as per [Foreign Exchange Management (Current Account Transactions Rules, 2000)] which prescribes the certain restrictions & limits on drawal of foreign exchange is governed by Schedules as mentioned below:

1. **Schedule-I** - Such transactions are strictly prohibited.

2. **Schedule-II** - & such transactions require prior approval of central government.

3. **Schedule-III** - Transactions which require prior approval of RBI, if such drawal exceeds specified limit of $2,50,000 under Liberalised Remittance Scheme.

(a) Mr. Rohan requires USD 1,20,000 for studies abroad on the basis of estimates given by foreign university. The same is covered under Schedule-III mentioned above, however, it is within the limit under LRS, but if Mr. Rohan requires higher amount for studies, he needs an estimation or approval letter from such institution. Therefore, Mr. Rohan does not require any prior approval in both cases as if drawal $1,20,000 or higher if such higher amt is given as an

Sure, here is the extracted handwritten text:

```
estimation by his institution or university.

(c) Mr. Rohan requires to remit $10,000
as gift. Such transaction is strictly
prohibited as per Schedule - I.
Therefore, Mr. Rohan cannot do so.

Ans 5 (c) A proxy is an instrument in
writing duly authorised a person
to appoint another person to
'attend' and 'vote' in a meeting on
his behalf.

As per Sec. 103 of the companies act,
2013. A person who is entitled to
vote in a meeting and right to
attend in a meeting appoints any
another person as a proxy
for his behalf.

Such proxy form have to be sub-
mitted with the company atleast
48 hours before the meeting.

In the instant case, Mr. X (member) appoints
Mr. Y (proxy) in prescribed form as on
11.04.2019 which is well within the time
limit specified.

Mr. W (member) appointed Mr. M (proxy)
on (12.04.2019) and Mr. N (proxy) on
(14.04.2019) By applying above prov-
isions, Mr. Y and Mr. M can be
```

treated as valid proxies as the form was
submitted within the prescribed time
limit.

Ans 5(a) As per the relevant provisions
of the companies act, 2013, if a person
wants to transfer its shares to another
person than it such person or who
transfer the shares and to which such
shares are being transferred apply
to the company to register such trans-
fer.

However, if the company fails to regi-
ster such transfer shall intimate
the parties within 30 days for such non-registration
and in such a case aggrieved party
shall apply to the NCLT.

In the above case, where shares are
transferred by Mr. Nilesh to his
sister Ms. Mukta. Company did not
register the transfer, and did not
send refusal notice.
In such a case, Ms. Mukta can
apply to NCLT, then NCLT may
pass an order and direct the co.
to register such transfer of
shares and asks reasons for such
contraventions.
